mapping.a_element: noisy channel from TF concentration to gene expression
mapping.b_element: noisy measurement channel from sparse signal to projected observations

mapping.a_element: mutual information between input and output
mapping.b_element: mutual information between signal and observations used in the converse bound

mapping.a_element: noise in TF binding and transcription

brief_justification: Both invoke Shannon mutual information across a noisy channel, but the actual machinery differs: paper A maximizes channel capacity over the input distribution and noise/dynamic-range matching to design an optimal scalar biological encoding, whereas paper B uses Fano-type converse bounds to derive necessary conditions on the number of measurements for high-dimensional combinatorial sparse-support recovery. The shared 'information-theoretic limits' vocabulary does not yield a coherent role-for-role mapping of one governing process, since one is a forward capacity-optimization and the other a converse sample-complexity lower bound on a structurally different (combinatorial, k-of-p) problem.
